---
title: "32 · The Projected Bellman Operator and MSPBE"
subtitle: "Geometrically: a wall, a target, and a Bellman push. On-policy the round-trip is a contraction; off-policy it need not be."
lesson-id: "mfrl.projected-bellman-operator"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.td-with-function-approximation", "mfrl.q-learning-with-fa", "t4.dot-cross-product", "t5.partial-derivatives"]
unlocks: ["mfrl.gradient-td"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you read on.

1. Can you state the **semi-gradient TD** update from
   [Lesson 29](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd) and say what
   "semi" means?
2. Can you compute a [dot product](/glossary.qmd#dot-product), and weight it
   by a probability distribution
   $\langle x, y \rangle_\mu = \sum_s \mu(s)\, x(s)\, y(s)$?
3. Can you name the three ingredients of the **deadly triad** from
   [Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)?
4. Do you know what a **$\gamma$-contraction** in a norm is — i.e. an
   operator $T$ with $\|Tu - Tv\| \le \gamma\,\|u - v\|$?

If any answer is no, revisit the named lesson or glossary entry before
continuing.
:::

## Touch

There are three states: $A$, $B$, $C$. Their true values are

$$v_\pi(A) = 1,\quad v_\pi(B) = 1,\quad v_\pi(C) = 3.$$

You don't have a table — you have *two knobs* $w_1$ and $w_2$, and your
estimate is forced to obey

$$\hat v(s) \;=\; w_1 \;+\; w_2 \cdot s,
\qquad s \in \{1, 2, 3\}\ \text{for}\ A, B, C.$$

Try a few settings of $(w_1, w_2)$. For each, write down $\hat v(A)$,
$\hat v(B)$, $\hat v(C)$, and the squared error
$E = (1 - \hat v(A))^2 + (1 - \hat v(B))^2 + (3 - \hat v(C))^2$.

| $w_1$ | $w_2$ | $\hat v(A)$ | $\hat v(B)$ | $\hat v(C)$ | $E$ |
|------:|------:|------------:|------------:|------------:|----:|
| $0$   | $1$   | $1$         | $2$         | $3$         | $1$ |
| $-1$  | $\tfrac{4}{3}$ | $\tfrac{1}{3}$ | $\tfrac{5}{3}$ | $3$ | $\approx 0.89$ |
| $-\tfrac{1}{3}$ | $1$ | $\tfrac{2}{3}$ | $\tfrac{5}{3}$ | $\tfrac{8}{3}$ | $\approx 0.67$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{3}$  | $1$ | $\tfrac{4}{3}$ | $\tfrac{7}{3}$ | $\tfrac{10}{3}$ | $\approx 2.0$ |

No setting hits all three targets exactly. The third row is the lowest error
you can reach.

::: {.lesson-beat}
There is no $(w_1, w_2)$ that makes $\hat v$ equal $v_\pi$. With two knobs
you can produce only a 2-D *plane* of possible $\hat v$ values inside the
3-D space $\mathbb{R}^3$. The truth lives off that plane. You can get
*closest to it*, but you cannot reach it.
:::

## Wonder

Sit with these. Don't write yet.

1. The set of all $\hat v$ you can produce by varying $(w_1, w_2)$ — what
   shape is it inside $\mathbb{R}^3$?
2. If we said state $B$ matters twice as much as $A$ or $C$, would the
   "best" setting of the knobs change?
3. The fitting rule is linear in $(w_1, w_2)$. Does that make finding the
   best setting easy, or just possible?
4. Suppose you don't know $v_\pi$; you only see TD targets
   $r + \gamma \hat v(s')$ — which themselves depend on $(w_1, w_2)$. Does
   the "best fit" idea still make sense when the target *moves*?

## Discover

Five problems. Each is small.

### Problem 1 — the feature span is a plane

Write each state's feature vector
$\phi(s) = (1, s)^\top$. Stack them as rows of a matrix

$$\Phi \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Then $\hat v = \Phi w$ where $w = (w_1, w_2)^\top$. Describe the set
$\{\Phi w : w \in \mathbb{R}^2\}$ as a subset of $\mathbb{R}^3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The two columns of $\Phi$ are the two feature vectors viewed across all
states. What does varying $w$ do to combinations of those columns?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\Phi w$ is a linear combination of the two columns of $\Phi$. Varying
$w \in \mathbb{R}^2$ traces out the **span** of those columns — a 2-D
subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$. We call this subspace the **feature span**
and write it $\mathcal{F} = \mathrm{span}(\Phi) \subset \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$.
Every realisable $\hat v$ lives on $\mathcal{F}$. Any $v$ off $\mathcal{F}$
— the true $v_\pi$ in the Touch — is not reachable by linear FA, no
matter how you tune $w$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — the closest point on the plane

Equip $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ with the $\mu$-weighted inner product

$$\langle x, y \rangle_\mu \;=\; \sum_s \mu(s)\, x(s)\, y(s),
\qquad \|x\|_\mu \;=\; \sqrt{\langle x, x \rangle_\mu}.$$

For any $v \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ there is a unique point $\Pi_\mu v$ in
$\mathcal{F}$ that minimises $\|v - \Pi_\mu v\|_\mu$. State (without proof)
why such a point exists and is unique, and write down what $\Pi_\mu v$ is
when $v$ already lies in $\mathcal{F}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You can pull two facts off the shelf from linear algebra: closed convex
subsets of a finite-dimensional inner-product space contain a unique
closest point to any external point; and projecting a point that's already
in the subspace gives the point itself.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathcal{F}$ is a finite-dimensional linear subspace; the $\mu$-weighted
inner product gives $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ a Hilbert-space structure (every
$\mu(s) > 0$ on the support). The orthogonal-projection theorem then
guarantees a unique $\Pi_\mu v \in \mathcal{F}$ minimising
$\|v - \cdot\|_\mu$. If $v \in \mathcal{F}$ then $\Pi_\mu v = v$ — the
closest point in a subspace to a point already in the subspace is itself.
**$\Pi_\mu$ is linear** (it's a composition of linear maps), and idempotent
($\Pi_\mu^2 = \Pi_\mu$).
</details>

### Problem 3 — projection is non-expansive

Show that for any $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$,

$$\|\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v\|_\mu \;\le\; \|u - v\|_\mu.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the Pythagorean theorem. The residual $v - \Pi_\mu v$ is orthogonal to
$\mathcal{F}$ in $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$. Drop the residual
component and see what remains.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Decompose $u - v = (\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v) + (\text{residual})$ where the
two parts are orthogonal in the $\mu$-norm. Pythagoras gives
$\|u - v\|_\mu^2 = \|\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v\|_\mu^2 + \|\text{residual}\|_\mu^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v \in \mathcal{F}$ (the subspace is closed under
subtraction). The residual $(u - \Pi_\mu u) - (v - \Pi_\mu v)$ is the
difference of two vectors each orthogonal to $\mathcal{F}$, so is itself
orthogonal to $\mathcal{F}$. The two pieces are orthogonal in
$\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$. By Pythagoras

$$\|u - v\|_\mu^2 \;=\; \|\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v\|_\mu^2
\;+\; \|(u - \Pi_\mu u) - (v - \Pi_\mu v)\|_\mu^2,$$

so $\|\Pi_\mu u - \Pi_\mu v\|_\mu^2 \le \|u - v\|_\mu^2$. **Projection
never lengthens distances** — it's *non-expansive* (1-Lipschitz) in the
norm whose inner product it's orthogonal to.
</details>

### Problem 4 — on-policy: the composition is a contraction

Recall from [L29 Stretch S1](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd#practice---stretch)
the result we cited without proof: when $\mu$ is the *on-policy stationary
distribution* of $\pi$, the Bellman operator $T_\pi$, defined by

$$(T_\pi v)(s) \;=\; \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma\, v(s')\bigr],$$

is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\|\cdot\|_\mu$: $\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_\mu \le
\gamma\, \|u - v\|_\mu$. Use that, plus Problem 3, to show that the
**composed operator** $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in
$\|\cdot\|_\mu$. Conclude using Banach's fixed-point theorem
([L11](../11-contraction-mapping-theorem/index.qmd)) that $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$
has a **unique fixed point** $v^\star \in \mathcal{F}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Chain the two Lipschitz bounds. Apply $T_\pi$ first, then $\Pi_\mu$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For any $u, v$,

$$
\|\Pi_\mu T_\pi u - \Pi_\mu T_\pi v\|_\mu
\;\le\; \|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_\mu \qquad \text{(Problem 3)}
$$

$$
\le\; \gamma\,\|u - v\|_\mu \qquad \text{(on-policy Bellman contraction)}.
$$

So $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction. Banach
([L11](../11-contraction-mapping-theorem/index.qmd)) gives a unique fixed
point $v^\star \in \mathcal{F}$ — the **TD fixed point** — and iteration
$v_{n+1} = \Pi_\mu T_\pi v_n$ converges geometrically from any starting
point. **This is what semi-gradient TD with linear FA on-policy actually
converges to** (Tsitsiklis–Van Roy 1997).
</details>

### Problem 5 — off-policy: the contraction can break

Suppose the data is generated by a *behaviour* policy $b$, not $\pi$. The
sampling distribution is then $\mu_b$, the stationary distribution of $b$,
not the on-policy $\mu_\pi$. The Bellman operator we still want to apply
is $T_\pi$ (we're evaluating $\pi$, not $b$). Argue informally — without a
proof — why $T_\pi$ need not be a $\gamma$-contraction in $\|\cdot\|_{\mu_b}$,
and conclude what this means for $\Pi_{\mu_b} T_\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The Bellman contraction on-policy uses the stationary property
$\sum_s \mu_\pi(s)\, p_\pi(s' \mid s) = \mu_\pi(s')$. The same identity
fails for $\mu_b$ paired with $T_\pi$'s transition kernel.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The on-policy proof of "$T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in
$\|\cdot\|_{\mu_\pi}$" leans on the fact that $\mu_\pi$ is *stationary
under $\pi$*: pushing mass forward by one step of $\pi$ does not change
$\mu_\pi$. Off-policy, the data distribution is $\mu_b$, but $T_\pi$
operates with $\pi$'s kernel. Those don't match. The cancellation that made
the contraction work no longer applies. Concretely you can construct
counterexamples where $\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_{\mu_b}$ exceeds
$\|u - v\|_{\mu_b}$ — i.e. $T_\pi$ *expands* the off-policy norm.

When the Bellman half of the round-trip is expansive, the projection half
can't always pull things back: $\Pi_{\mu_b} T_\pi$ need not be a
contraction, the fixed point need not be unique (or even exist), and the
iteration $v_{n+1} = \Pi_{\mu_b} T_\pi v_n$ can diverge. **This is the
deadly triad in one picture**: function approximation (the projection),
bootstrapping (the Bellman operator), and off-policy data (the wrong
weighting) together break the contraction argument.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The composition $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ is exactly what John Tsitsiklis and Benjamin
Van Roy used in their 1997 *IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control* paper
"An Analysis of Temporal-Difference Learning with Function Approximation"
to give the first rigorous convergence proof for TD(0) with linear FA.
The proof is essentially Problem 4 — the rest of the paper handles the
stochastic-approximation bookkeeping that gets you from "fixed point of an
operator" to "limit of the stochastic update". Twenty-eight years later
their conditions still define the boundary of safe.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The projection is a **wall**: $\hat v$ can never leave the feature span.
The Bellman update wants to push the value somewhere on the other side
of the wall; the projection drags it back. On-policy weighting keeps that
drag *short enough* — at most $\gamma$-shrink per round-trip — that the
dance converges to a single point. Off-policy weighting lets the
drag *grow*: the Bellman push can expand, and the projection can no
longer fix it. Divergence becomes possible — not certain, but no longer
ruled out.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

Let $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{|S| \times d}$ be the feature matrix and
$\mathcal{F} = \{\Phi w : w \in \mathbb{R}^d\}$ the **feature span**. Let
$\mu$ be a probability distribution on $S$ with $\mu(s) > 0$ for all $s$
in the support.

The **[projection operator](/glossary.qmd#projected-bellman-operator)
$\Pi_\mu : \mathbb{R}^{|S|} \to \mathcal{F}$** sends each $v$ to the unique
closest point in $\mathcal{F}$ under the $\mu$-weighted norm. In matrix
form,

$$\Pi_\mu \;=\; \Phi\,(\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi)^{-1}\,\Phi^\top D_\mu,
\qquad D_\mu = \mathrm{diag}(\mu).$$

The **projected Bellman operator** is the composition
$\Pi_\mu T_\pi$. Its fixed points in $\mathcal{F}$ are the TD-style
solutions.

The **[mean-square projected Bellman error](/glossary.qmd#mspbe) (MSPBE)**
of a weight vector $w$ is

$$\mathrm{MSPBE}(w) \;=\; \bigl\|\,\hat v_w \;-\; \Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w\,\bigr\|_\mu^2.$$

Its zeros are exactly the TD fixed points: $\mathrm{MSPBE}(w^\star) = 0$
iff $\hat v_{w^\star} = \Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_{w^\star}$.

:::

In one mnemonic line:

> *The projection is the wall; the Bellman is the push; on-policy the
> round-trip shrinks.*

## Apply

### Worked example — the 3-state chain from Touch

States $A$, $B$, $C$ with $s = 1, 2, 3$; true values $v_\pi = (1, 1, 3)^\top$;
features $\phi(s) = (1, s)^\top$; uniform weighting $\mu = (\tfrac13,
\tfrac13, \tfrac13)$.

**Step 1: build $\Phi$ and $D_\mu$.**

$$\Phi = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3\end{pmatrix},
\qquad D_\mu = \tfrac{1}{3}\, I_3.$$

**Step 2: compute the projection matrix.**

$$\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi
= \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 \\ 2 & \tfrac{14}{3}\end{pmatrix}.$$

Its inverse is $\frac{1}{1 \cdot \tfrac{14}{3} - 4}
\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{14}{3} & -2 \\ -2 & 1\end{pmatrix}
= \frac{3}{2}\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{14}{3} & -2 \\ -2 & 1\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}7 & -3 \\ -3 & \tfrac{3}{2}\end{pmatrix}.$

**Step 3: project $v_\pi$.**

The least-squares weights are $w^\star = (\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi)^{-1}\,
\Phi^\top D_\mu\, v_\pi$. Compute

$$\Phi^\top D_\mu\, v_\pi = \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}5 \\ 12\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{5}{3} \\ 4\end{pmatrix},$$

then

$$w^\star = \begin{pmatrix}7 & -3 \\ -3 & \tfrac{3}{2}\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{5}{3} \\ 4\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{35}{3} - 12 \\ -5 + 6\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}-\tfrac{1}{3} \\ 1\end{pmatrix}.$$

So the projection is

$$\Pi_\mu v_\pi \;=\; \Phi w^\star
= \begin{pmatrix}-\tfrac{1}{3} + 1 \\ -\tfrac{1}{3} + 2 \\ -\tfrac{1}{3} + 3\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{2}{3} \\ \tfrac{5}{3} \\ \tfrac{8}{3}\end{pmatrix}.$$

**Step 4: check the residual is orthogonal.**

The residual is $v_\pi - \Pi_\mu v_\pi = (\tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{2}{3},
\tfrac{1}{3})^\top$. Its $\mu$-weighted inner product with
$(1, 1, 1)^\top$ (the first feature) is
$\tfrac{1}{3}(\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}) = 0$. With
$(1, 2, 3)^\top$ (the second feature):
$\tfrac{1}{3}(\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{4}{3} + 1) = 0$. **The residual is
orthogonal to $\mathcal{F}$ in $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$**, as
the theory promised.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** With features $\phi(s) = (1, s)^\top$ on three states and uniform
$\mu$, what is $\Pi_\mu v$ for $v = (1, 2, 3)^\top$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$v$ lies on the feature span: try $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v = (1, 2, 3) = \Phi (0, 1)^\top$, so $v \in \mathcal{F}$ and $\Pi_\mu v
= v$. Projecting a point already in the subspace returns the point itself
(idempotency).
</details>

**W2.** Same features, same $\mu$. What is $\Pi_\mu v$ for $v = (1, 1, 1)^\top$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$v$ is the all-ones vector; that's $\Phi (1, 0)^\top$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v = \Phi (1, 0)^\top$, so $v \in \mathcal{F}$ and $\Pi_\mu v = v$.
Constant vectors are always in the span when one feature is the bias
$\phi_1 = 1$.
</details>

**W3.** Write the matrix $\Pi_\mu$ in full for the worked example.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\Pi_\mu = \Phi (\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^\top D_\mu$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Using the pieces from the worked example:

$$\Pi_\mu = \Phi \begin{pmatrix}7 & -3 \\ -3 & \tfrac{3}{2}\end{pmatrix}
\Phi^\top D_\mu.$$

Multiplying out:

$$\Pi_\mu = \tfrac{1}{6}\begin{pmatrix}
5 & 2 & -1 \\
2 & 2 & 2 \\
-1 & 2 & 5
\end{pmatrix}.$$

Row sums are $1$ (consistent with $\Pi_\mu \mathbf 1 = \mathbf 1$). The
matrix is idempotent: $\Pi_\mu^2 = \Pi_\mu$ — you can check by
re-multiplying. It is **not** symmetric in the standard sense; it is
self-adjoint with respect to $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$, which
means $D_\mu \Pi_\mu = (D_\mu \Pi_\mu)^\top$. Verify:
$D_\mu \Pi_\mu = \tfrac{1}{18}\begin{pmatrix}5 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ -1
& 2 & 5\end{pmatrix}$ is symmetric.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Re-do the worked example with weighting $\mu = (\tfrac12, \tfrac14,
\tfrac14)$. Does the best $w$ change? Compute $\Pi_\mu v_\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The least-squares normal equations are
$\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi\, w = \Phi^\top D_\mu\, v_\pi$. Rebuild both sides.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
With $D_\mu = \mathrm{diag}(\tfrac12, \tfrac14, \tfrac14)$:
$\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi = \begin{pmatrix}1 & \tfrac{7}{4} \\ \tfrac{7}{4} &
\tfrac{15}{4}\end{pmatrix}$, $\Phi^\top D_\mu v_\pi = \begin{pmatrix}
\tfrac{3}{2} \\ \tfrac{11}{4}\end{pmatrix}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Solving the $2 \times 2$ system:
$\det = \tfrac{15}{4} - \tfrac{49}{16} = \tfrac{11}{16}$. Then
$w^\star = \tfrac{16}{11}\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{15}{4} & -\tfrac{7}{4} \\
-\tfrac{7}{4} & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{3}{2} \\
\tfrac{11}{4}\end{pmatrix}$. Working through: $w_1^\star = \tfrac{16}{11}
\cdot \tfrac{1}{4}(\tfrac{15 \cdot 3}{2} - \tfrac{7 \cdot 11}{4}) =
\tfrac{16}{11} \cdot \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot \tfrac{90 - 77}{4}
= \tfrac{16 \cdot 13}{11 \cdot 16} = \tfrac{13}{11}$. And $w_2^\star =
\tfrac{16}{11} \cdot \tfrac{1}{4}(-\tfrac{21}{2} + 11)
= \tfrac{16}{11} \cdot \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}
= \tfrac{2}{11}$. **Change of $\mu$ changes the projection.** Heavier
weight on $A$ pulls the fit closer to $v_\pi(A) = 1$, lighter weight on
$C$ lets the fit miss $v_\pi(C) = 3$ by more.
</details>

**P2.** A student writes: "The projection $\Pi$ is the same matrix no
matter what $\mu$ we use; only the *measure of error* changes." Refute in
one short paragraph.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compare W3 and P1.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Different $\mu$ give different projections — see P1 vs the worked example,
where $\Pi_\mu v_\pi$ changed from $(\tfrac{2}{3}, \tfrac{5}{3}, \tfrac{8}{3})$
to $(\Phi w^\star)$ with $w^\star = (\tfrac{13}{11}, \tfrac{2}{11})$. The
formula $\Pi_\mu = \Phi (\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^\top D_\mu$
literally has $D_\mu$ in it. "Project onto the feature span" is incomplete
as a recipe — *in what norm?* must be specified. The norm chooses which
errors matter, and that choice is encoded in $\mu$.
</details>

**P3.** State the relationship between $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ and **semi-gradient
TD** (Lesson 29). What does semi-gradient TD converge to in the
on-policy linear-FA regime?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use Problem 4 (Discover) and Tsitsiklis–Van Roy (the fun-fact).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tsitsiklis–Van Roy (1997) showed that, under linear FA and on-policy
sampling with Robbins–Monro step sizes, semi-gradient TD's parameter
$w_t$ converges (with probability 1) to the unique $w^\star$ satisfying
$\hat v_{w^\star} = \Pi_{\mu_\pi} T_\pi \hat v_{w^\star}$. In words: the
algorithm finds the **TD fixed point** — the fixed point of the projected
Bellman operator $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ — which is exactly where MSPBE($w$) is
zero. This *names* what we said in L29 was happening without naming.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Construct an off-policy weighting $\mu_b$ on 2 states that makes
the Bellman operator $T_\pi$ *expansive* in $\|\cdot\|_{\mu_b}$ — i.e.
find $u, v$ with $\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_{\mu_b} > \|u - v\|_{\mu_b}$. Use
$\gamma = 0.9$ and any deterministic $\pi$. This is the off-policy half of
the deadly-triad story.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Take two states where $\pi$ sends almost all mass to one state, but $\mu_b$
puts almost all weight on the other. The Bellman operator pushes mass
forward by $\pi$; if $\mu_b$ doesn't match, the norm can magnify the
difference.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Two states $\{1, 2\}$. Policy $\pi$ goes $1 \to 2$ and $2 \to 2$
(absorbing). On-policy $\mu_\pi$ puts most mass on state 2. Choose
$\mu_b = (0.99, 0.01)$ — opposite of stationary. Take $u = (1, 0)$,
$v = (0, 0)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $\pi$: $T_\pi v(s) = r(s) + \gamma\, v(\pi(s))$ on this deterministic
setup (taking $r = 0$ for simplicity). So $T_\pi u(s_1) = 0.9 \cdot u(2)
= 0$ and $T_\pi u(s_2) = 0.9 \cdot u(2) = 0$. Thus $T_\pi u = (0, 0) =
T_\pi v$. That gives $\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_{\mu_b} = 0$ — a contraction
of $u$ to itself, no expansion. We need a different setup.

Try $\pi$: $1 \to 1$, $2 \to 1$. Then $T_\pi u = (0.9 \cdot u_1, 0.9 \cdot
u_1)$. Pick $u = (1, 0)$, $v = (0, 0)$. $T_\pi u = (0.9, 0.9)$, $T_\pi v
= (0, 0)$. With $\mu_b = (0.01, 0.99)$:
$\|u - v\|_{\mu_b}^2 = 0.01 \cdot 1 + 0.99 \cdot 0 = 0.01$, so
$\|u-v\|_{\mu_b} = 0.1$.
$\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_{\mu_b}^2 = 0.01 \cdot 0.81 + 0.99 \cdot 0.81 = 0.81$,
so $\|T_\pi u - T_\pi v\|_{\mu_b} = 0.9$.

Ratio $0.9 / 0.1 = 9 \gg \gamma$. **The Bellman operator expanded by a
factor of 9 in this off-policy norm.** Compose with projection and the
fixed-point iteration can diverge.

The intuition: state 1 is rare under $\mu_b$, but it's where $\pi$ sends
everything. The Bellman push amplifies "what does the value at state 1
look like" — and $\mu_b$ has nearly stopped measuring state 1.
</details>

**S2.** Show that the *Bellman error* $\|T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w\|_\mu^2$
and the *projected* Bellman error $\|\Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w\|_\mu^2$
are different objectives. Which one does semi-gradient TD secretly
descend?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Decompose $T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w = (T_\pi \hat v_w - \Pi_\mu T_\pi
\hat v_w) + (\Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w)$. The two pieces are
orthogonal in $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$ because $\hat v_w$ and
$\Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w$ both live in $\mathcal{F}$ while the first piece
is in $\mathcal{F}^\perp$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Pythagoras in $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mu$:

$$\|T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w\|_\mu^2 \;=\;
\underbrace{\|T_\pi \hat v_w - \Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w\|_\mu^2}_{\text{distance to }\mathcal{F}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\|\Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w\|_\mu^2}_{\mathrm{MSPBE}(w)}.$$

The first term measures how far the Bellman image is from the feature span
— a quantity that depends only on the model and the features, *not on
$w$*. The second term is exactly MSPBE. So the Bellman error decomposes
as a $w$-independent constant plus MSPBE. **Semi-gradient TD descends
MSPBE (when it converges), not the full Bellman error.** That distinction
is what makes semi-gradient TD work despite the moving target — it's
chasing a $w$-meaningful objective, not a fictitious one. It is also why
*residual gradient* (full SGD on $\|T_\pi \hat v_w - \hat v_w\|^2$) finds
the wrong fixed point in mis-specified models: the residual-gradient
objective is contaminated by the $w$-independent first term.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\Pi$ is just the closest point geometrically."** No — *geometry*
   here depends on $\mu$. With Euclidean weighting you get one closest
   point; with $\mu$-weighted you get another. Two different "closest"
   answers from two different inner products. The same feature span; the
   same target $v$; different projections.
2. **"MSPBE is just the squared TD error."** No. The TD error is $\delta$;
   summing $\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta^2]$ gives the **mean-square TD error
   (MSTDE)**, *not* MSPBE. MSPBE measures distance to the *projected*
   target, which is the only meaningful thing when the target lives off
   the feature span. The two objectives have different minimisers and
   different gradients — gradient-TD ([L33](../33-gradient-td/index.qmd))
   exists precisely to descend MSPBE rather than MSTDE.
3. **"Off-policy plus FA always diverges."** No. The **deadly triad**
   needs **all three**: FA, bootstrapping, off-policy data. Off-policy
   *Monte Carlo* (no bootstrapping) with FA converges. Off-policy
   bootstrapping with *tabular* (no FA) converges. Tabular plus
   bootstrapping plus off-policy (Q-learning) converges. Mix all three
   and the contraction argument breaks — but breaking is *possible*, not
   *necessary*. DQN runs the deadly triad daily and mostly works.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **The feature span is a wall.** Your estimate $\hat v_w$ can never
   leave it; the best you can hope for is the projection of the truth
   onto that wall.
2. **On-policy, the round-trip shrinks; off-policy, it can grow.** The
   projection is always non-expansive, but the Bellman operator is only
   a contraction in the *on-policy* norm. Match the weighting to the
   policy and convergence follows; mismatch them and divergence becomes
   possible.
3. **MSPBE is the objective that knows about the wall.** Semi-gradient
   TD secretly descends MSPBE — that's what its fixed point is. The next
   lesson builds the algorithm that *actually* descends MSPBE by honest
   gradient.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you sketch (on paper) why $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction
   on-policy, without notes?
2. Did you compute the projection in P1 with a different $\mu$ and watch
   the answer change?
3. Can you explain — to a friend — why the *deadly triad* needs all three
   ingredients to bite, and which one each existing convergence theorem
   relaxes?

- **3 yes:** continue to
  [Lesson 33 — Gradient-TD](../33-gradient-td/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo S2, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-derive Problem 4 by hand and replay Problem 5.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.td-with-function-approximation](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)** —
  the algorithm whose fixed point we just named. Stretch S1 of L29
  *mentioned* the projection; this lesson promotes it to a named object.
- **[mfrl.q-learning-with-fa](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)** — the
  lesson where the deadly triad first appears. Problem 5 above explains
  *why* the triad bites: the Bellman operator stops being a contraction
  in the off-policy norm.
- **[t4.dot-cross-product](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)** —
  inner products, orthogonality, the basis for the $\mu$-weighted norm.
- **[t5.partial-derivatives](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)** —
  needed to derive the normal equations $\Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi\, w = \Phi^\top D_\mu\, v$.

### What this unlocks

- **[Gradient-TD: GTD, GTD2, TDC](../33-gradient-td/index.qmd)** — the
  family of algorithms that descends MSPBE by honest gradient, breaking
  the deadly triad's curse at the cost of variance and a second parameter.
- Retroactively, this lesson **names** what
  [L29 Stretch S1](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd#practice---stretch)
  and [L30 Problem 3](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd) were really
  about — the projected Bellman operator, $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$, the silent
  protagonist of the entire FA arc.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every modern value-based algorithm — DQN, Double-DQN, Rainbow,
SAC's critic — is implicitly minimising MSPBE-ish objectives in feature
spaces defined by neural networks. The deadly-triad reasoning above is why
those algorithms need engineering tricks (target networks, replay buffers,
double Q) to stay stable: they live in the regime where the clean
contraction argument no longer applies.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The projected Bellman operator is the central object of
*approximate dynamic programming theory*. Bertsekas's two-volume *Dynamic
Programming and Optimal Control* (2017) develops convergence theorems for
ADP algorithms by analysing fixed points of $\Pi T$ under various
projections — including non-Euclidean ones (averagers, aggregators).
Sutton & Barto Chapter 11 also organises the deadly-triad discussion
around MSPBE.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
sketches the theoretical-RL view from 30,000 feet; MSPBE and the
projected Bellman operator are the two objects that arc draws into
sharper focus.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §8.3–§8.4 (projected Bellman, MSPBE). Cite freely;
  never transcribe Zhao's prose.
- Also cross-referenced: Sutton & Barto, *Reinforcement Learning: An
  Introduction* (2nd ed.), §11.4 (the geometry of linear value-function
  approximation) for the wall/push picture; Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1997)
  for the on-policy convergence proof; Bertsekas, *Dynamic Programming
  and Optimal Control, Vol. II* (4th ed.), Chap. 6 for the projected
  Bellman framework.
- Touchstones honoured: 3Blue1Brown visual intuition (the wall and the
  push), Better Explained "Aha!" framing (the round-trip
  contraction-or-expansion picture), Gelfand-style problem-first
  sequencing (the contraction theorem is *derived* through Problems 3–4
  before being named).
:::
